Fiber Type Selection
OM3 Multimode
MMF · AQUA
▶
SpeedMax ReachNotes
10GbE300m10GBASE-SR, LC duplex
25GbE70mSFP28 SR, limited
40GbE100mQSFP+ SR4, MPO-12
100GbE70mQSFP28 SR4, MPO-12
400GbE50mQSFP-DD SR8, MPO-16
OM3 = aqua jacket. Being phased out in new builds — prefer OM4.
OM4 Multimode
MMF · MAGENTA
▶
SpeedMax ReachNotes
10GbE550m10GBASE-SR
25GbE100mSFP28 SR
40GbE150mQSFP+ SR4
100GbE100mQSFP28 SR4
400GbE100mQSFP-DD SR8 / OSFP SR8

Erika violet (magenta) jacket. Standard for in-rack and short cross-connect runs.

OM5 Wideband Multimode
MMF-WB · LIME
▶
SpeedReachNotes
100G SWDM4150mLC duplex — 4 wavelengths
400G SWDM8150mLC duplex possible

Lime green jacket. Backward compat with OM4 at OM4 distances. LC duplex at 100G+.

OS2 Single-Mode
SMF · YELLOW
▶
SpeedMax ReachOptic
10GbE10km / 80kmLR / ER / ZR
25GbE10kmSFP28 LR
100GbE10 / 40 / 80kmLR4 / ER4 / ZR4
400GbE2 / 10kmDR4 / FR4 / LR4
400GbE80km+ZR / ZR+ (coherent DWDM)
NEVER mix APC (green, 8° angle) + UPC (beige, flat) — ~60dB insertion loss. Link will not work.
LC Connector — Field Reference
LC · DUPLEX
▶

LC (Lucent Connector) is the standard in data center fiber work. Small form factor, push-pull latch, 1.25mm ferrule. Used on SFP/SFP+/SFP28 and some QSFP breakout cables.

PropertyDetail
Ferrule diameter1.25mm (vs SC/ST at 2.5mm)
Connector typeDuplex standard — Tx on one, Rx on other
Polish typesUPC (beige/blue body) · APC (green body, 8° angle)
Typical jacketAqua = OM3 · Magenta = OM4 · Yellow = OS2
Insertion loss<0.3dB typical · <0.1dB with good clean
Return loss UPC≥50dB
Return loss APC≥60dB — preferred for SMF long haul
Polarity — Duplex LC
SideFunctionVerify
Strand A (top)Tx on one end = Rx on far endCheck with VFL (red laser)
Strand B (bottom)Rx on one end = Tx on far endLight out = Tx confirmed
Common Field Mistakes
MistakeSymptomFix
Flipped duplex (Tx→Tx)No RX light, DOM shows –40dBmFlip one end of duplex pair
APC into UPC portHigh insertion loss, ~60dBMatch polish type — check ferrule color
Dirty end-faceMarginal Rx, FEC errors risingDry clean → inspect → wet/dry if needed
Pull tab stuckCan't extract — latch not releasingUse LC tool or depress latch fully before pulling
Bend radius violationIntermittent signal, no obvious causeInspect routing — min bend radius 15× cable OD
ℹ UPC and APC ferrules look identical at a glance — always check the connector body color. Green = APC. Beige/blue = UPC. Mixing them destroys return loss even if the link comes up.
IEC 61300-3-35 — Fiber Inspection
PASS/FAIL
▶

The international standard for end-face inspection pass/fail. Every fiber cleaning and inspection decision should reference these zones. Used by VIAVI FiberChek, Fluke FI-7000, and all major inspection probes.

The Four Inspection Zones — Single-Mode (UPC)
ZoneRadiusScratchesDefects / Particles
A — Core0–25µmNONE allowedNONE allowed
B — Cladding25–120µmNone >2µm wideNone >5µm
C — Adhesive120–250µmMinor OK<10 particles, none >10µm
D — Contact250–125µm ODNone crossing A or BMinor OK
Multimode (OM3/OM4/OM5) Zones
ZoneRadiusScratchesDefects
A — Core0–25µmNONE allowedNONE allowed
B — Cladding25–120µmNone >5µmNone >10µm
C — Adhesive120–250µmAllowedAllowed, no bridging
D — Contact250–125µm ODAllowedAllowed
What Each Failure Means
Failure ZoneRoot CauseAction
Zone A scratchDirty cleave tool, contaminated cleaner, over-insertionRe-clean — if persists, replace connector
Zone A particleDirty environment, touched end-face, blown dustDry clean → re-inspect. Wet/dry if still present
Zone B scratchAbrasive contamination on cleanerRe-clean with fresh cleaner — never re-use tape
Zone B particleResidue from previous cleaning fluidWet clean with IPA → dry → inspect
Zone C/D onlyNormal handling, minor environmentalPASS — document and proceed
Cleaning Protocol
StepActionTool
1Dry clean first — never skipOne-click cleaner or dry tape
2Inspect immediately afterVIAVI FiberChek / Fluke FI-7000
3If fail → wet/dry cleanIPA pen → dry cleaner
4Inspect again — max 3 cyclesSame probe
5Still fail → escalate or replaceDocument with probe screenshot
NEVER blow on a fiber end-face — breath moisture + CO₂ = contamination. Never touch the ferrule. Never re-use cleaning tape. Always cap unused connectors immediately after inspection.
Transceiver Form Factors
SFP / SFP+ / SFP28 / SFP56
1G–50G
▶
FormSpeedBackward Compat
SFP1GbE—
SFP+10GbESFP (1G only)
SFP2825GbESFP+ in cage
SFP5650GbE—
ℹ SFP+ and SFP28 are physically identical — verify speed on port config.
QSFP+ / QSFP28 / QSFP-DD / OSFP
40G–800G
▶
FormSpeedLanesNotes
QSFP+40G4×10GBreaks to 4×10G
QSFP28100G4×25GBreaks to 4×25G
QSFP56200G4×50G PAM4—
QSFP-DD400G8×50G PAM4QSFP28 compat
QSFP-DD800G8×100G PAM4Next-gen
OSFP400/800G8 lanesDiff cage — NOT compat
MPO / MTP Polarity
Polarity Methods A / B / C
CRITICAL
▶

Wrong polarity = no light on any lane. #1 MPO installation error.

MethodTrunkUse Case
AStraight (key-up to key-down)SR4/SR8 — most common
BFlipped (key-up to key-up)With crossover adapters
CPair-flippedBiDi / 8-fiber apps
Verify polarity before pulling trunk. Replacing trunk in conduit is expensive.
HPC / GPU Compute Stack
IB NDR / NVLink 5.0
GB300
▶
StandardSpeedCableReach
NDR InfiniBand400Gb/sQSFP112 / OSFP2m DAC / 100m AOC
HDR InfiniBand200Gb/sQSFP562m DAC / 100m AOC
NVLink 5.01.8TB/sCu NVLinkIn-chassis only
Ethernet spine400GbEQSFP-DD / OSFPPer optic
ℹ NVLink 5.0 on GB300 NVL72 is fully internal. External fabric = OSFP NDR InfiniBand.
Rx Optical Power (dBm)
10G SR OM3/OM4
–7.3 to –1
ALARM LOW: –11.1 dBm
25G SR OM4
–8.4 to +2.4
ALARM LOW: –11.4 dBm
100G SR4 OM4
–9.5 to +2.4
ALARM LOW: –11.5/lane
100G LR4 SMF
–10.6 to +4.5
ALARM LOW: –14.4/lane
400G SR8 OM4
–9.5 to +2.4
8 lanes total
400G DR4 SMF
–9.3 to +2.3
4 lanes @ 100G
–40 dBm = NO SIGNAL. Near HIGH ALARM = attenuate or check bend radius.
Temperature & Electrical
Normal Op Temp
0° to 70°C
Commercial grade
Temp Warning
75°C
Check airflow
Temp Alarm
80°C
Tx shuts down
Supply Voltage
3.135 –3.465V
Alarm: <3.0 or >3.6V
Tx Bias Normal
2–100 mA
Normal range
Bias Alarm
>120 mA
Aging laser — replace
Rising Tx bias = laser nearing end of life. Flag for proactive replacement before it drops production.

Arista EOS
EOS

show interfaces Ethernet1/1 transceiver
show interfaces transceiver
watch 5 show interfaces Eth1/1 transceiver

MULTI

show interface ethernet 1/1 transceiver details
show interface ethernet 1/1 transceiver | include alarm
# Linux / ConnectX-7
ethtool -m ens1f0
mlxlink -d /dev/mst/mt4129_pciconf0 -m

Cable Reference
DAC Cable — Direct Attach Copper
PASSIVE / ACTIVE
▶
What Is a DAC?

A DAC (Direct Attach Copper) cable is a fixed-assembly copper twinax cable with transceivers permanently attached at each end. It plugs directly into QSFP, QSFP-DD, or SFP+ ports and does not use fiber. No optic, no DOM light readings — it transmits electrically.

Passive vs Active
TypeReachPowerDOMUse Case
Passive DAC≤3m (typically 1–3m)~0.1WLimited — no Tx/Rx powerToR to spine, NVLink node-to-node
Active DAC3–10m~0.5–1WMore complete DOMLonger rack-to-rack runs
DOM Behavior — Critical Field Note
Passive DAC cables do NOT report Rx power via DOM. A reading of –40 dBm or "N/A" on a DAC link is NORMAL. Do not condemn the cable based on Rx DOM alone.

Active DACs may report partial DOM. Tx power is usually present. Rx power may show as 0 or absent. Always verify link state via interface counters, not DOM alone.

Speed / Form Factor Matrix
Form FactorSpeedCommon Use
SFP+ DAC10GLegacy ToR, management
QSFP+ DAC40GH100 NVSwitch links, older spine
QSFP28 DAC100GConnectX-7, Arista 7050CX3
QSFP-DD DAC400GGB300 NVLink, Arista 7800
OSFP DAC400G / 800GNext-gen spine, QM9700
Troubleshooting DAC Links
CHECK 01
Verify link state — not DOM
Run show interfaces on Arista or equivalent. Check input/output errors and FEC counters. DAC DOM is unreliable for fault diagnosis.
CHECK 02
Re-seat both ends
DAC connectors can work loose under cable weight. Pull and firmly re-seat both ends. Verify latch is engaged.
CHECK 03
Check bend radius
DAC twinax is stiffer than fiber. Tight bends near the connector cause signal degradation. Minimum bend radius: 4× cable diameter.
CHECK 04
Verify vendor compatibility
DAC cables are subject to the same vendor lock as optics on Arista. Run service unsupported-transceiver if the DAC is not recognized.
Never use a DAC where reach exceeds its rated distance. Signal integrity degrades rapidly beyond spec. At 400G, even 0.5m over spec can cause uncorrectable FEC errors.
AOC Cable — Active Optical Cable
OPTICAL
▶
What Is an AOC?

An AOC (Active Optical Cable) is a fiber cable with active electrical-to-optical conversion circuitry permanently built into each connector. It looks like a DAC but uses fiber inside. It plugs into the same QSFP/SFP ports as a DAC but transmits optically, giving it much greater reach.

DAC vs AOC — When to Use Which
DACAOC
Reach≤10m1–100m
PowerLowerHigher (~1–2W)
WeightHeavierLighter
Bend radiusStifferMore flexible
DOMLimitedFull Tx/Rx power
Best forSame-rack, ≤3mCross-rack, ≤100m
DOM on AOC

AOCs report full DOM including Tx and Rx power. A healthy AOC typically shows Rx in the –1 to –7 dBm range depending on cable length. Use DOM thresholds from the DOM/DDM tab for alarm reference.

Troubleshooting AOC Links
CHECK 01
Check DOM Rx power
Unlike DAC, AOC Rx power is meaningful. Rx below –14 dBm on a short AOC run is a red flag. Could indicate physical damage to the internal fiber.
CHECK 02
Inspect for kinks
AOC internal fiber is fragile. Any sharp kink or excessive bend can break fibers inside the jacket with no external sign of damage. Check the full cable run.
CHECK 03
AOC is not field-repairable
Unlike discrete optic + fiber, AOC is a single assembly. If the internal fiber breaks, replace the entire cable. No splice, no clean, no fix.
Do not use AOC in high-temperature environments without verifying the cable's operating temperature spec. Active circuitry in the connector is temperature-sensitive.
SC Connector — Field Reference
SC · DUPLEX
▶
SC Overview

SC (Subscriber Connector) is a push-pull fiber connector with a 2.5mm ferrule. Older than LC but still common in legacy infrastructure, campus runs, and some OOB management links. You will encounter it in inherited environments.

SCLC
Ferrule diameter2.5mm1.25mm
Connection stylePush-pull latchDuplex clip
SizeLargerSmaller — preferred in DC
Common useLegacy, campus, OOBSFP+, QSFP, all modern DC
PolarityBlue = UPC, Green = APCBlue = UPC, Green = APC
APC vs UPC on SC
NEVER connect SC-APC (green, 8° angled) to SC-UPC (blue, flat). The angled ferrule will not seat correctly and will cause ~60dB insertion loss. The link will not work and you risk scratching both ferrules.
Cleaning SC Connectors

SC ferrules are 2.5mm — use 2.5mm cleaning tools, not 1.25mm LC tools. IEC 61300-3-35 zones apply identically. Zone A (core area) must be contamination-free.

SC to LC Adapter

SC-to-LC hybrid adapters exist for connecting legacy SC runs to modern LC-based equipment. Verify the adapter is rated for the wavelength in use. Insertion loss through a hybrid adapter is typically 0.3–0.5 dB additional.

ℹ If you encounter SC connectors in a modern GPU data center they are almost certainly on legacy OOB management links, console runs, or inherited infrastructure — not on production HPC fabric.
RJ45 — Ethernet Pinout & Field Reference
8P8C · COPPER
▶
What Is RJ45?

RJ45 is the common name for an 8P8C (8-position, 8-contact) modular connector used for twisted-pair Ethernet. Eight conductors arranged in four twisted pairs. Found on every server's OOB management port, IPMI/BMC interface, and most 1G management aggregation links. Locking tab on top when looking at the contacts.

T568B Pinout — DC Standard
PinPairColor10/100 Use1G/10G Use
12Orange/WhiteTX+BI_DA+
22OrangeTX-BI_DA-
33Green/WhiteRX+BI_DB+
41Blue—BI_DC+
51Blue/White—BI_DC-
63GreenRX-BI_DB-
74Brown/White—BI_DD+
84Brown—BI_DD-
ℹ T568B is the de-facto standard in U.S. data centers. Check existing infrastructure before terminating new runs — mixing T568A and T568B in a single link creates a crossover and breaks the connection.
T568A vs T568B

The two standards differ only in pairs 2 and 3 (orange and green swap positions). Both work for Ethernet — what matters is consistency at both ends. T568A is more common in residential and U.S. government installs; T568B is the default in commercial and DC environments.

A T568A jack on one end and T568B on the other = a crossover cable. Modern equipment with Auto-MDIX will handle this, but older switches and out-of-band serial consoles may not. If a link won't come up, verify both ends use the same standard.
T568A Pinout — Reference
PinPairColor10/100 Use1G/10G Use
13Green/WhiteRX+BI_DB+
23GreenRX-BI_DB-
32Orange/WhiteTX+BI_DA+
41Blue—BI_DC+
51Blue/White—BI_DC-
62OrangeTX-BI_DA-
74Brown/White—BI_DD+
84Brown—BI_DD-
ℹ T568A and T568B are functionally identical — pairs 1 and 4 are unchanged, only pairs 2 and 3 swap positions. Auto-MDIX masks the difference on most modern gear, but consistency at both ends is still the safe practice.
Pin Orientation — Plug View vs Jack View

Both pinout tables above are plug view (the standard reference): hold the RJ45 plug with the contacts facing you and the locking tab pointing AWAY (down, behind your fingers). Pin 1 is on the LEFT.

ViewHolding PositionPin 1 Location
Plug viewContacts toward you, locking tab down/backLeft side
Jack view (looking into port)Looking into the female port from the frontRight side (mirrored)
Punch-down view (110/keystone block)Block facing you, top side upTop — but check the block label
Pin 1 flips depending on whether you're looking at a plug, a jack, or a punch-down block. When in doubt, trace the orange-white wire — in T568B it's always pin 1, in T568A it's always pin 3. The wire color is the unambiguous reference.
Category Ratings
CatSpeedBandwidthMax ReachDC Use
Cat5e1 Gbps100 MHz100mLegacy OOB only
Cat61G / 10G short250 MHz100m / 55m at 10GCommon OOB / IPMI
Cat6a10 Gbps500 MHz100mModern OOB / 10G mgmt
Cat710 Gbps600 MHz100mRare — proprietary GG45
Cat825 / 40 Gbps2000 MHz30mToR-to-server short runs
Field Troubleshooting
CHECK 01
Inspect the connector
Bent, missing, or pushed-back pins are the most common RJ45 failure. Look down into the jack with a flashlight. Pins should be evenly aligned at the front edge.
CHECK 02
Verify pair ordering with a tester
A cheap RJ45 cable tester ($15–25) lights up each conductor in sequence. Wrong pair order, missed crimp, or split pair will show immediately.
CHECK 03
Check for split pairs
A split pair passes a continuity test but fails at gigabit due to crosstalk. Symptom: link comes up at 100 Mbps but not 1G. Re-terminate following T568B exactly.
CHECK 04
Verify cable rating for the speed
A Cat5e cable will negotiate to 1G but not reliably to 10G. If a 10G OOB link is flapping, check the cable spec printed on the jacket.
Never terminate RJ45 with the jacket pulled too far back. The twist must be preserved up to within ~12.5mm (½") of the contacts. Untwisted pairs cause crosstalk and intermittent failures that are nearly impossible to diagnose remotely.
Console Cable — Cisco Rollover (RJ45 Serial)
RJ45 · NOT ETHERNET
▶
What Is a Rollover Cable?

A rollover cable looks identical to an Ethernet patch cable — RJ45 connectors on both ends — but the wiring is completely different. It's an RS-232 serial cable carrying console data, not Ethernet. The "rollover" comes from the fact that pin 1 on one end maps to pin 8 on the other end, pin 2 to pin 7, and so on. Cisco devices ship with a flat light-blue rollover cable; the color is the visual cue.

Plugging a rollover cable into a regular Ethernet port will not damage the equipment, but the link will never come up. Conversely, plugging an Ethernet cable into a console port will not give you serial access. Identification by color alone is unreliable — verify pinout if there's any doubt.
Rollover Pinout
End A PinEnd B PinFunction
18RTS / CTS
27DTR / DSR
36TxD / RxD
45Ground
54Ground
63RxD / TxD
72DSR / DTR
81CTS / RTS
Default Console Settings — Cisco / Arista / MLNX
SettingValue
Baud rate9600 (most) / 115200 (some Arista, Mellanox, GB300 BMC)
Data bits8
ParityNone
Stop bits1
Flow controlNone
ℹ Shorthand: 9600 8N1 or 115200 8N1. If you see garbage characters, baud is wrong — try 115200 first on modern gear.
Modern Adapters

Most laptops no longer have RS-232 DB9 ports, so the field-standard combo is: RJ45 rollover cable + DB9-to-USB serial adapter (or a USB-to-RJ45 console adapter that combines both into one cable). FTDI and Prolific are the two common chipsets — FTDI is more reliable on macOS and Linux, Prolific often needs driver workarounds.

Some cheap USB-to-serial adapters use counterfeit FTDI chips. Genuine FTDI drivers can detect these and disable the chip ("brick" it). If your adapter stops working after a driver update, this is likely why — replace with a known-good adapter.
Field Troubleshooting
CHECK 01
Verify cable type by color or label
Cisco rollovers are typically flat light blue. If unmarked, a tester showing all 8 conductors crossed (1↔8, 2↔7, etc.) confirms it's a rollover.
CHECK 02
Try both common baud rates
If 9600 produces garbage, try 115200. Modern Arista, Mellanox switches, and GB300 BMC interfaces typically use 115200. Cisco IOS legacy is 9600.
CHECK 03
Check the USB adapter is enumerating
macOS: ls /dev/tty.usb*. Linux: dmesg | tail after plug-in to see if a /dev/ttyUSB0 was created. Windows: Device Manager → Ports (COM & LPT).
CHECK 04
Confirm flow control is disabled
If the terminal opens but typed characters don't echo, flow control is likely set to hardware (RTS/CTS) instead of none. Set to none in the terminal app (screen, minicom, PuTTY).
DC Power Cables — IEC & NEMA Field Reference
POWER · LIVE
▶
Why This Matters

Plugging the wrong power cable into the wrong PDU is at best a non-event (it doesn't fit) and at worst a tripped breaker, popped fuse, or damaged equipment. Typically you'll see C13/C14 on most servers, C19/C20 on high-draw H100/H200 GPU compute and PDU outputs, and 208V 3-phase service to the rack PDUs — confirm your site's PDU spec. Know the family before you plug.

Never force a power connector. If it doesn't seat with light pressure, it's wrong. Forcing a C13 into a C19 socket is impossible; forcing a NEMA L6-20 into an L6-30 will damage the receptacle and may create an arc fault.
IEC 60320 Family — Server & PDU
PlugReceptacleRatingCommon Use
C13C1410A / 250VMost 1U–2U servers, switches, console servers
C15C1610A / 250V (high-temp)Rare in DC — kettle/server in hot environments
C19C2016A / 250VHigh-draw servers, GPU nodes, PDU inputs, blade chassis
C21C2220A / 250V (high-temp)Rare — high-draw + thermal margin
ℹ Mnemonic: even-numbered = receptacle (the female socket on the equipment / PDU). Odd-numbered = plug (the male end on the cable). C13 plugs into C14. C19 plugs into C20.
Locking IEC — C13/C14 with P-Lock or Locking Latch

Standard C13 connectors can pull loose under cable weight or vibration. Locking variants (Schurter V-Lock, P-Lock, Tripp Lite Plug-Lock) physically retain the plug in the socket via a small mechanical latch. They look like normal C13/C14 but require a button-press or twist to release. Common on dense PDU outputs feeding GPU servers where an accidental unseat = lost rack.

Locking C13s do not interoperate across vendors. A Schurter V-Lock plug fits a standard C14 socket (good), but a standard C13 plug will rattle loose in a Schurter V-Lock socket because the locking notch isn't engaged.
NEMA — North American Branch Circuits
TypeVoltageAmpsCommon DC Use
5-15125V15AStandard wall outlet — office gear, KVM cart
5-20125V20AHigher-draw single-phase 120V — rare in DC
L5-20125V20A lockingLocking 120V — small DC PDUs
L6-20250V20A lockingCommon DC PDU input — twist-lock
L6-30250V30A lockingHigh-draw rack PDUs — twist-lock
L15-30250V 3-phase30A locking3-phase rack PDU input
L21-30208Y/120V 3-phase30A lockingWye 3-phase rack PDU input
IEC 60309 — International / High-Power 3-Phase

IEC 60309 (sometimes called "pin-and-sleeve") is the international standard for industrial power connectors. Color-coded by voltage: blue for 200–250V single-phase, red for 380–480V 3-phase, yellow for 100–130V. The clock-position of the keyway encodes voltage and frequency — a 415V 50Hz plug will not seat in a 480V 60Hz socket. Common at GB300 NVL72 PDU inputs (60A, 415V 3-phase red).

ColorVoltageCommon DC Spec
Blue200–250V single-phase16A / 32A — older PDU input
Red380–480V 3-phase32A / 60A / 100A — GPU pod PDU input (415V typical)
Yellow100–130VRare in DC — North American jobsite tools
Field Troubleshooting
CHECK 01
Verify the receptacle before pulling the plug
Many DC PDUs have similar-looking C13 vs C19 sockets in close proximity. Confirm receptacle type by reading the molded label on the PDU before reaching in. Pulling the wrong plug = downtime on a different server.
CHECK 02
Check for breaker trip before swapping cables
If a server lost power and the cable looks fine, check the upstream PDU breaker first. A swapped cable on a tripped circuit is wasted effort and can mask the real fault.
CHECK 03
Verify A-feed / B-feed before unplugging
Dual-corded servers have redundant A and B power. Pulling one is safe IF the other is verified live. Confirm both PSU LEDs are green on the server before touching either cord.
CHECK 04
Twist-lock NEMA — twist to release
L5/L6/L15/L21 plugs are twist-lock. Pulling straight will not release them and may damage the receptacle. Rotate counterclockwise ~⅛ turn until you feel the lock disengage, then pull. If you have to fight it, you're twisting the wrong direction.
CHECK 05
3-phase — never disconnect under load without verification
A 415V 3-phase plug carries enough current to arc dramatically if pulled hot under heavy load. Confirm the downstream PDU is in a known state (typically zero output if upstream branch is dead) before disconnecting any 60309 connector.
Power work has the highest blast-radius of anything a DCT touches. When in doubt, lock-out / tag-out and call electrical. A wrong move on the optical side breaks one link. A wrong move on the power side can take down a quarter-row.
Hardware Reference
H100 / H200 LED Reference — Supermicro 8U
SYS-821GE · AS-8125GS
▶
Scope

This reference covers two specific Supermicro HGX platforms: SYS-821GE-TNHR (HGX H100 8-GPU 8U) and AS-8125GS-TNHR (HGX H200 8-GPU 8U). LED conventions overlap heavily across both — differences are called out explicitly. Always cross-reference with current Supermicro docs for the firmware version on your specific node.

LED meanings can shift slightly between BMC firmware versions. This card is a starting point — never condemn or RMA hardware based on LED state alone. Confirm with IPMI sensor data, dmesg, and nvidia-smi before any destructive action.
Rack Position — Typical Site Convention

Typical placement: H100 and H200 nodes two per rack at fixed U positions — confirm your site's convention. Bottom-U reference convention (U number is the chassis's lowest occupied U).

PositionBottom UOccupied U RangeNotes
Lower nodeU12U12 – U19Reach from floor / typical kneeling height
Upper nodeU26U26 – U33Eye-level / standing reach for most techs
ℹ U1–U11 below the lower node holds switches, PDU mounts, and cable management. U20–U25 is the inter-node gap (cable routing + PDU access). U34–U48 above the upper node holds the second-feed PDU and additional cable management. Memorize the U positions — when called to "the bottom GPU node in R-12," U12 is your starting point.
Front Panel — System Status
LEDStateMeaning
PowerSolid greenSystem on, AC and DC OK
PowerOffSystem off OR no AC input — check both PSUs
System StatusSolid greenPOST complete, OS running, no faults
System StatusSolid amberNon-fatal warning — open IPMI System Event Log
System StatusBlink amberCritical fault — sensor over-threshold, fan failure, or thermal event
UID (Unit ID)Solid blueLocator beacon active — toggled via IPMI or front-panel button
Network ActivityBlink greenOnboard mgmt NIC traffic — does NOT indicate GPU NICs
ℹ The UID LED is your friend in a hot aisle. Trigger it via IPMI before walking to the rack: ipmitool chassis identify 60 lights it solid blue for 60 seconds. Critical for finding the right node in a dense pod.
Power Supplies — PSU LEDs

Both platforms ship with redundant Titanium-rated PSUs (3000W+ per unit on H100, 5250W+ on H200). Each PSU has a single multi-color LED on the rear face.

StateMeaningAction
Solid greenAC input present, output OK, PSU healthyNormal — no action
Blink green (1Hz)AC present, PSU in standby (system off)Normal when system is powered down
Solid amberPSU failure — predictive (warning) or currentSchedule replacement; run ipmitool sensor to confirm
Blink amberPSU warning — temp high, fan slow, or input voltage marginalInvestigate before it escalates to solid amber
OffNo AC input OR PSU deadVerify power cord and breaker; if both OK, PSU is faulted
Both PSUs must be the same model and wattage. Mixing PSU SKUs in a redundant pair will cause one or both to refuse to provide output. Check the part number printed on the PSU body.
GPU Module — HGX Baseboard LEDs

The HGX H100/H200 baseboard sits behind the front bezel. LEDs on the baseboard itself are not visible without service-mode chassis access, but each GPU has a status LED visible through the chassis vents on most Supermicro 8U designs. Inspect with a flashlight from the rear if the front bezel is in place.

LEDStateMeaning
GPU Status (per GPU)Solid greenGPU initialized, NVLink up, present in nvidia-smi
GPU Status (per GPU)Solid amberGPU present but degraded — XID error, ECC fault, or NVLink down
GPU Status (per GPU)Off (when system is on)GPU not initialized — nvidia-smi will show "GPU has fallen off the bus" or missing entry
NVSwitch (where exposed)Solid greenNVSwitch healthy, all NVLink lanes negotiated
NVSwitch (where exposed)AmberNVSwitch fault or degraded NVLink lane — check fabric manager logs
ℹ For deeper GPU diagnosis, do not rely on LEDs alone. Run nvidia-smi -q for per-GPU detail and dmesg | grep NVRM for XID error codes. LEDs only tell you "something is wrong" — the logs tell you what.
NIC LEDs — Onboard & ConnectX

The 8-GPU HGX systems ship with multiple ConnectX-7 (H100) or ConnectX-8 (H200) NICs in addition to the onboard 1G management NIC. LED conventions:

NIC TypeLink LEDActivity LED
Onboard 1G mgmtGreen = link up at 1G; Amber = 100M; Off = no linkBlink green = traffic
ConnectX-7 200G/400GGreen = link at advertised speed; Amber = link at degraded speedBlink yellow/green = traffic
ConnectX-8 400G/800GGreen = full link speed; Amber = degraded; Off = no linkBlink = traffic
A green link LED only confirms physical link. It does NOT confirm the optic is matched to the spec, that LACP has formed, or that traffic is actually flowing. Always cross-check with ip link show and ethtool before signing off on a port.
Drive Bays — NVMe LEDs

Both platforms support NVMe drive bays in the front panel. Per-bay LED conventions:

StateMeaning
Solid greenDrive present, healthy, idle
Blink greenDrive activity — read or write in progress
Solid amberDrive predictive failure (SMART warning) — schedule replacement
Solid redDrive failed — RMA candidate, rebuild may be in progress
Blink blueLocate beacon active — set via storcli or BMC for visual identification
OffNo drive present OR drive not detected by HBA
BMC / IPMI Network Port
LEDStateMeaning
LinkSolid greenBMC NIC has link to mgmt switch
LinkOffBMC NIC has no link — check cable, mgmt switch port, BMC power state
ActivityBlink amber/yellowBMC traffic — IPMI session, SOL, or remote KVM
ℹ The BMC has its own power rail and stays alive when the host is powered down (as long as AC is present). If BMC link is up but you can't reach it via IPMI, check the BMC IP config — not the host network.
Critical Field Patterns
PATTERN 01
All green, no traffic
System healthy and idle. Normal during off-shift or between training jobs.
PATTERN 02
Solid amber System Status, all PSUs green
Non-fatal warning likely from sensors (temp, fan speed). Pull IPMI SEL: ipmitool sel list. Address before it escalates.
PATTERN 03
Blinking amber System Status + one PSU amber
PSU failure with system still running on the redundant pair. SAFE to replace the faulted PSU hot — but verify the surviving PSU is rated to carry full load alone before pulling.
PATTERN 04
One GPU amber, system green
Single-GPU fault. Run nvidia-smi and grep for XID errors in dmesg. Common causes: ECC error count exceeded, NVLink lane down, thermal event recovered. Often warrants a node drain + reboot, sometimes RMA.
PATTERN 05
All GPU LEDs off, system on
PCIe enumeration failure or HGX baseboard fault. nvidia-smi will show no GPUs. Reboot first — if persistent, escalate. Possibly an HGX baseboard or NVSwitch failure, which is a full chassis service event.
PATTERN 06
Both PSUs solid amber simultaneously
DO NOT pull either PSU. Both are reporting a fault — the system may be running on stored capacitance or about to lose power. Verify upstream PDU / branch circuit immediately. Investigate before any cable touch.
H100/H200 systems pull 3–10 kW under load. Hot-swapping PSUs or pulling cables under heavy training load can trigger emergency power-off events that lose hours of training state. When in doubt, drain the workload first.
Optical Safety — Laser Class & Inspection Rules
EYE SAFETY
▶
Why This Matters

Data center optics emit infrared laser light at wavelengths your eye cannot see. The pupil does not contract in response to invisible light, which means there's no protective reflex — the laser can damage retinal tissue silently. A single brief look into an active fiber port can cause permanent vision loss with no immediate pain. This is not a theoretical risk; it has happened to working DCTs.

Never look directly into a fiber port, transceiver, or fiber end face that may be active. "May be active" includes any optic still seated in a powered switch, any fiber connected at the far end to a powered device, and any fiber whose status you have not personally verified as dark.
Laser Classification — IEC 60825-1
ClassRiskCommon DC Encounters
Class 1Safe under all reasonable conditionsMost short-reach optics (DR4, SR4, 100G-SR) — sealed in transceiver, eye-safe by design
Class 1MSafe to the unaided eye, hazardous with magnificationSome longer-reach optics — DO NOT use a fiber inspection scope without confirming the link is dark first
Class 3RLow risk, but direct viewing can cause damageSome longer-reach 10G/100G optics, certain test equipment lasers
Class 3BHazardous to direct viewing — eye injury possibleLong-reach DWDM, OTDR test equipment, some 400G ZR optics
Class 4Hazardous including diffuse reflectionsExtremely rare in standard DC fiber — research / scientific use
Most data center optics are Class 1 or Class 1M, but you cannot tell the class by looking at the transceiver. The class is in the spec sheet. When in doubt, treat every fiber as potentially hazardous and never look directly into it.
Common Wavelengths in DC Fabric
WavelengthVisible?Common Use
850 nmNo (near-IR)Short-reach multimode — SR, SR4, SR8 (most common in DC)
1310 nmNo (near-IR)Single-mode short — DR, DR4, FR, FR4, LR, LR4
1550 nmNo (near-IR)Single-mode long — ER, ER4, ZR, DWDM
650 nmYes (red)Visual Fault Locator (VFL) — the only laser you can see, intentionally visible for fiber tracing
ℹ The fact that you cannot see light coming out of a fiber means NOTHING about whether the laser is on. 850/1310/1550 nm are all invisible. A "dark" fiber to your eye may be carrying full-power infrared.
Safe Inspection — The Rules
RULE 01
Confirm the link is down at the source
Before inspecting any fiber end face, verify the upstream switch port is admin-down via CLI: show interface Eth1/1. The port must show "administratively down" — link-down due to lost peer is NOT enough; the laser may still be transmitting.
RULE 02
Use a fiber inspection scope, never your eye
A USB or handheld fiber scope (200×–400× magnification) provides imaging without exposing your eye. Industry standard tools include the Fluke FI-7000 series or Lightel DI-1000. The scope's optics block laser light to the eyepiece.
RULE 03
Never use a regular microscope
A standard microscope or magnifying glass focuses the laser beam directly into your eye. Class 1M optics that are normally safe become Class 3B-equivalent under magnification. Use only laser-safe fiber inspection tools.
RULE 04
Cap unused fiber ends
Every disconnected fiber end gets a dust cap immediately. Caps protect the end face from contamination AND block any stray light from a still-active far end. If you don't have a cap, do not leave the fiber unattended.
RULE 05
Treat OTDRs and VFLs with extra care
OTDRs (Optical Time-Domain Reflectometers) emit Class 1M to Class 3B pulses — never look into the test port or the far end of a fiber under test. Visual Fault Locators (VFL) are 650nm red laser at typically Class 2 — visible but still capable of eye damage on direct prolonged exposure. Do not aim a VFL at anyone's eyes.
RULE 06
If exposed — get medical attention immediately
Suspected laser eye exposure is a medical emergency. Symptoms may not appear for hours but damage is immediate and permanent. Do not "wait and see." Document the laser source, exposure duration, and report through your site safety chain. Emergency rooms can refer to ophthalmology with retinal imaging capability.
Optical safety is the one place where "I've done this a thousand times without incident" reasoning fails catastrophically. The hazard accumulates silently. Every fiber port gets the same caution every time, regardless of how routine the task feels.
Fiber Inspector Cleaning — IEC 61300-3-35

The IEC 61300-3-35 standard defines acceptance criteria for fiber end-face inspection by zone (core, cladding, adhesive, contact area). A modern fiber scope automatically grades the end face per this standard with a pass/fail.

ZoneDiameter (SM)Tolerance
A — Core0–25 μmZero defects of any size
B — Cladding25–120 μmLimited defects allowed by size and count
C — Adhesive120–130 μmMost defects acceptable
D — Contact130–250 μmLargest tolerance; most defects acceptable
ℹ A failed Zone A inspection means "do not connect" — even a single particle in the core can cause severe link loss or permanent end-face damage when mated under pressure. Clean (don't connect dirty), re-inspect, then mate.
Down Link — Fiber Triage Protocol

Work top-down. Confirm each step before advancing. Never skip physical layer.

STEP 01 — PHYSICAL
Inspect the physical layer first
• Cable fully seated? Pull-tab blocking lock?
• Bend radius violations? Fiber crimped?
• Cracked ferrule or dirty end-face?
• Correct polarity on duplex LC (Tx→Rx)?
• MPO: correct key-up / key-down?
STEP 02 — CLEAN END-FACE
Clean fiber before any DOM check
IEC 61300-3-35 pass/fail standard.
• Zone A (0–25μm): ZERO contamination
• Zone B (25–120μm): No scratches >2μm
• Zone C (120–250μm): Minor particulates OK
Dry first, wet/dry if needed. Clean before re-inspect.
Tool: VIAVI FiberChek · Fluke FI-7000 · Thorlabs
STEP 03 — DOM Rx POWER
Pull real-time Rx power
LOW ALARM or worse: dirty fiber, wrong type, bad splice, dead Tx.
HIGH ALARM: attenuator needed or wrong optic class.
show interfaces Ethernet1/1 transceiver show interface eth1/1 transceiver details mlxlink -d /dev/mst/mt4129_pciconf0 -m
STEP 04 — OPTIC COMPATIBILITY
Confirm optic is vendor-qualified
Arista QSFP vendor lock — bypass with service unsupported-transceiver + key.
Check: speed, reach, fiber type (MMF vs SMF), DDM support.
STEP 05 — FEC & ERROR COUNTERS
Degrade vs. hard-down
• Rising FCS/CRC = marginal signal
• Input errors + no FCS = local RX hardware
• Flapping resets = intermittent physical
• FEC uncorrectable > 0 = escalate now
show interfaces Ethernet1/1 counters errors
STEP 06 — LOOPBACK
Isolate local vs. remote fault
Loopback at your end → port healthy, fault is remote.
Loopback at remote → cable and ports good, check config.
test cable-diagnostics tdr interface Ethernet1/1
STEP 07 — SWAP & DOCUMENT
Cable → optic → port. One at a time.
Log: original part, swap part, result, timestamp.
Tag & bag failed optic: port, date, symptoms. Do not discard.
Common Failure Patterns
Link up — dropping packets
MARGINAL
▶

Dirty fiber or marginal optic. Link negotiates but BER elevated.

  • Check Rx power — within sensitivity window?
  • Rising FEC correctable errors over time?
  • Clean both ends of the fiber run
  • Check for micro-bend at cable management points
Do not leave a high-FEC-correctable link in production without scheduling maintenance. It will fail.
Rx shows –40 dBm or LOS
HARD DOWN
▶
  • –40 dBm = zero light — not just low
  • Check far-end Tx power: is it transmitting?
  • Verify continuity with visible light source (VLS)
  • Check polarity: Tx lane going into Rx?
  • MPO: flipped key? Wrong trunk polarity (A/B/C)?
  • Broken strand: OTDR to locate break distance
LOS on both ends simultaneously = cable break, not optic failure.
Interface flapping / resets climbing
INTERMITTENT
▶
  • Thermal: Tx power varies with temp — reseat, observe
  • Dirty connector: particulates causing intermittent LOS
  • Loose LC: check clip lock is engaged
  • Cable stress at patch panel entry
  • Bad QSFP/SFP: swap optic first
60-second flaps = LACP/keepalive timer. Physical flaps are irregular.
Optic not recognized by switch
COMPAT
▶
  • Arista: look for "Not supported" in transceiver output
  • Check vendor lock — Arista requires key for 3rd-party
  • EEPROM blank: presents as unknown even if approved
  • Try known-good same-type optic to isolate port vs. optic
switch(config)# service unsupported-transceiver <KEY>
switch(config)# interface ethernet 1/1 vendor-override
IB / NDR
OOB
Console
Test & Cert
Parser
Cable Certifier — Fluke DTX

Certification proves a cable plant meets TIA-568 standards. Required for warranty claims, insurance, and commissioning sign-off. Know what you're certifying and what the results mean.

Link Test vs Channel Test
IMPORTANT
▶
Test TypeWhat It IncludesWhen to Use
Link (Permanent Link)Horizontal cable only — no patch cordsCertifying installed cable plant. Standard for data center.
ChannelHorizontal cable + both patch cordsEnd-to-end certification including active equipment patch cords
ℹ Data centers almost always certify to Permanent Link. Know which your project requires before testing — they use different adapters on the Fluke.
Reading DTX Results
RESULTS
▶
ResultMeaningAction
PASSAll parameters within TIA limitDocument, move on
PASS*Marginal — within limit but closeDocument asterisk. Monitor. May fail under load or temp change.
FAILOne or more parameters out of specIdentify failing test, remediate, re-certify
Key Parameters
ParameterWhat It ChecksCommon Fail Cause
Wire MapCorrect pin-to-pin continuitySplit pair, crossed pair, mis-termination
LengthCable not exceeding 90m (permanent link)Run too long, VoP calibration off
Insertion LossSignal attenuation over lengthDamaged cable, bad termination, excessive length
NEXTNear-End Crosstalk between pairsUntwisted pairs at termination, split pairs
Return LossSignal reflection back to sourceImpedance mismatch, kinked cable
Propagation DelaySignal travel timeExcessive length, wrong cable category
Delay SkewTiming difference between pairsShould be <45ns for Cat6A
Common Fail Causes & Fixes
TROUBLESHOOT
▶
FailureLikely CauseFix
Wire map failSplit pair or crossed terminationRe-terminate both ends. Check pair grouping.
NEXT failToo much untwisted at keystone/punchKeep untwist to <13mm (Cat6) / <6mm (Cat6A)
Insertion loss failRun over 90m, damaged cableMeasure actual run. Replace damaged section.
Return loss failKink in cable, bad keystoneInspect full run. Replace keystone.
PASS* on NEXTMarginal terminationRe-terminate with tighter untwist. Re-test.
Never exceed 90m for horizontal permanent link. With 5m patch each end = 100m channel max. Velcro too tight and zip ties on fiber both cause return loss failures.
Fiber Certification — OLTS

Optical Loss Test Set (OLTS) certifies fiber attenuation meets TIA-568 / IEC 14763-3 loss budgets. Different tool from the copper certifier.

OLTS Pass/Fail Thresholds
FIBER
▶
Fiber TypeWavelengthMax Loss / kmConnector Budget
OM3/OM4 MMF850nm3.5 dB/km0.75 dB per mated pair
OM3/OM4 MMF1300nm1.5 dB/km0.75 dB per mated pair
OS2 SMF1310nm0.4 dB/km0.75 dB per mated pair
OS2 SMF1550nm0.3 dB/km0.75 dB per mated pair

Total loss budget = fiber loss + (connector pairs × 0.75dB) + splice loss. If measured loss exceeds budget: dirty connectors, bad splice, or damaged fiber.

ℹ Always clean both ends before OLTS measurement. A dirty connector adds 0.5–3dB alone and will cause a false fail.
Multimeter in the DC
What to Measure & Where
SAFETY FIRST
▶
WhatWhereExpected Reading
Single-phase 208VHot to neutral at receptacle200–220V AC
3-phase line-to-lineL1–L2, L2–L3, L1–L3~208V AC each
3-phase line-to-neutralEach leg to neutral~120V AC each
Circuit dead checkHot to ground after breaker off0V — if not 0V, stop
ContinuityCable ends, ground pathBeep = continuous
SAFETY RULES — READ BEFORE USING METER IN DC:
• Use CAT III rated meter minimum for PDU receptacle work
• One hand in pocket when probing live circuits — prevents current path across your heart
• Never probe inside an open PDU cabinet near bus bars — that's an electrician's job
• Check probe leads for cracks or exposed wire before every use
• Verify meter is in correct mode (ACV not DCV) before touching probes to AC circuit
• If you see arcing, smell burning, or breaker trips when you connect — stop immediately
DCT Scope vs Electrician Scope
KNOW YOUR LIMITS
▶
TaskDCTElectrician
Measure voltage at receptacle✓ OK
Verify circuit is dead at outlet✓ OK
Continuity check on cable✓ OK
Measure current on PDU branchWith training
Open PDU cabinet panel✗ NO✓
Work near bus bars✗ NO✓
Replace breaker✗ NO✓
Terminate hardwired feeds✗ NO✓
Quantum-2 QM9700 InfiniBand
ibstat / perfquery / UFM
▶
ibstat
ibstatus
ibnetdiscover
perfquery -x <LID> <port>
ibnodes | grep -i down
perfquery -R <LID> <port>
NDR IB is highly sensitive to signal integrity. NVIDIA-qualified AOC or DAC only.
What is OOB?

Out-of-Band management is a dedicated network path to server BMCs and switch management ports that is physically separate from the production data network. When in-band is completely dead — OS crashed, NIC failed, network misconfigured — OOB is how you get in without touching hardware.

ℹ OOB should be on a separate switch, separate VLAN, and ideally a separate UPS leg from production. If OOB shares a failure domain with production, it fails when you need it most.
Access Methods
Dell iDRAC
DELL
▶
# SSH to iDRAC directly
ssh admin@<idrac-ip>

# racadm — remote management CLI
racadm -r <idrac-ip> -u admin -p <pw> getsysinfo
racadm -r <idrac-ip> -u admin -p <pw> serveraction powercycle
racadm -r <idrac-ip> -u admin -p <pw> getsel

# ipmitool via iDRAC
ipmitool -I lanplus -H <idrac-ip> -U admin -P <pw> chassis status
ipmitool -I lanplus -H <idrac-ip> -U admin -P <pw> sol activate

# Web UI
https://<idrac-ip> → Virtual Console → Serial

# iDRAC default port: 443 (HTTPS), 22 (SSH), 623 (IPMI)
# Default creds (change immediately): root / calvin
iDRAC 9 requires modern TLS. If browser rejects cert, use Firefox or racadm CLI. Chrome may block self-signed iDRAC certs.
HPE iLO
HPE
▶
# SSH to iLO
ssh administrator@<ilo-ip>

# iLO CLI commands
show system1
reset /system1
show /system1/log1/entry*

# ipmitool works with iLO too
ipmitool -I lanplus -H <ilo-ip> -U administrator -P <pw> sel list

# Redfish REST API
curl -k -u admin:<pw> https://<ilo-ip>/redfish/v1/Systems/1

# Default port: 443. Default creds: administrator / (printed on server pull tab)
Supermicro IPMI
SUPERMICRO
▶
# Web UI at port 443
https://<ipmi-ip> → Remote Control → iKVM

# ipmitool (same as others)
ipmitool -I lanplus -H <ipmi-ip> -U ADMIN -P ADMIN sel list
ipmitool -I lanplus -H <ipmi-ip> -U ADMIN -P ADMIN power status
ipmitool -I lanplus -H <ipmi-ip> -U ADMIN -P ADMIN power reset

# Default creds: ADMIN / ADMIN (change immediately)
# Some units: ADMIN / (last 8 chars of MAC address)
NVIDIA HGX / GB200 / GB300 BMC
NVIDIA
▶
# Redfish API — primary management interface
curl -k -u admin:<pw> https://<bmc-ip>/redfish/v1/Systems
curl -k -u admin:<pw> https://<bmc-ip>/redfish/v1/Systems/System_0/LogServices/EventLog/Entries

# ipmitool still works on NVIDIA BMC
ipmitool -I lanplus -H <bmc-ip> -U admin -P <pw> sdr list
ipmitool -I lanplus -H <bmc-ip> -U admin -P <pw> sel list

# GPU health via BMC (HGX specific)
curl -k -u admin:<pw> https://<bmc-ip>/redfish/v1/Systems/System_0/Processors

# BMC cold reset (when BMC itself is unresponsive)
ipmitool -I lanplus -H <bmc-ip> -U admin -P <pw> mc reset cold
If BMC is completely unresponsive: physical BMC reset button on server backplane. Check server service manual for location — varies by OEM integrator (Foxconn, Wiwynn, QCT).
Finding the BMC IP When Unknown
DISCOVERY
▶
  • Check DCIM — BMC IPs should be documented. If not, that's a gap.
  • ipmitool locally on the OS: ipmitool lan print 1 — shows BMC IP if OS is alive
  • Check DHCP server lease table for the BMC MAC address (usually printed on service tag)
  • Aruba/Cisco OOB switch: show arp on the OOB switch VLAN — BMC will have an ARP entry if it has a lease
  • Dell: racadm getsysinfo locally on OS shows iDRAC IP
  • HPE: hponcfg -g locally shows iLO IP
If BMC has no IP at all: it may be set to static and misconfigured, or DHCP exhausted. Connect via physical serial console to configure.
OOB Network Architecture
DESIGN
▶
ComponentStandardNotes
OOB switchDedicated 1G switchSeparate from production fabric. Never shared.
OOB VLANDedicated VLAN (e.g. VLAN 100)Routed but firewalled. No production traffic.
OOB subnetRFC 1918 privateCommonly 10.0.0.0/24 or 192.168.x.0/24 per site
OOB powerSeparate UPS legIdeally different PDU from servers it manages
Speed1G copperBMC ports are 1G. Never need more.
AccessJump host / VPNNever directly internet-routable
Console Cable Reference

When in-band and OOB are both down, physical serial console is your last resort. Know this before you need it.

Baud Rates by Platform
CRITICAL
▶
PlatformBaudSettings
Cisco IOS / NX-OS96008N1, no flow control
Arista EOS96008N1, no flow control
NVIDIA MLNX-OS96008N1, no flow control
Dell iDRAC serial1152008N1, no flow control
HPE iLO serial96008N1, no flow control
Supermicro IPMI1152008N1, no flow control
NVIDIA HGX BMC1152008N1, no flow control
Linux (GRUB/kernel)1152008N1, console=ttyS0,115200
Linux (legacy)9600Older distros may default 9600
Garbage on screen = wrong baud rate. Try 115200 first, then 9600, then 38400. Never change other settings (8N1 is universal).
Cisco Rollover Cable Pinout
PINOUT
▶

The Cisco rollover (yost) cable is the standard for most network gear console ports. RJ45 to DB9.

RJ45 PinSignalDB9 Pin
1RTS8
2DTR6
3TxD2
4GND5
5GND5
6RxD3
7DSR4
8CTS7
ℹ Rollover = pin 1 connects to pin 8, pin 2 to pin 7, etc. — each pair is reversed. Not the same as an Ethernet straight-through cable.
USB-to-Serial Adapter Guide
ADAPTERS
▶
ChipsetOSStatus
FTDI FT232macOS 10.9+Built-in driver. Plug and play.
FTDI FT232LinuxBuilt-in. Device: /dev/ttyUSB0
FTDI FT232WindowsDownload from ftdichip.com
Prolific PL2303macOS 12+Broken — use FTDI instead
Prolific PL2303Windows 10/11Often works but less reliable than FTDI
CP2102 (Silicon Labs)AllGood alternative to FTDI
Avoid cheap Prolific clones on macOS — driver is broken on Monterey and later. Buy FTDI-based adapters only for reliable DC work.
Terminal Emulator Settings
SOFTWARE
▶
# macOS / Linux — screen
screen /dev/ttyUSB0 9600
screen /dev/tty.usbserial-XXXX 115200
# Exit screen: Ctrl-A then K

# macOS / Linux — minicom
minicom -s ← configure port + baud first
minicom -b 9600 -D /dev/ttyUSB0

# Linux — find your serial device
dmesg | grep tty
ls /dev/ttyUSB*
ls /dev/ttyACM*

# PuTTY (Windows) settings:
Connection type: Serial
Serial line: COM3 (check Device Manager)
Speed: 9600 or 115200
Data bits: 8, Stop bits: 1, Parity: None, Flow: None
Console Cable Types by Gear
REFERENCE
▶
GearConsole PortCable Needed
Cisco switches/routersRJ45RJ45 rollover + DB9 adapter
Arista switchesRJ45RJ45 rollover + DB9 or USB-A
MLNX switchesRJ45RJ45 rollover + USB adapter
Dell PowerEdgeDB9 or USB mini-BDB9 null modem or USB mini-B
HPE ProLiantDB9 or USBDB9 null modem or USB-A
SupermicroDB9DB9 null modem cable
NVIDIA HGX serversUSB Type-A or RJ45Check OEM (Wiwynn/Foxconn/QCT) manual
ℹ Carry: 1x RJ45 rollover cable, 1x DB9-to-USB adapter (FTDI), 1x USB-A cable. That covers 95% of DC console situations.
Output Parser & Interpreter

Paste raw CLI output, port map, or fiber data. Parser identifies type, extracts key data, flags issues.

Detect type or select:
AUTO
DOM
Errors
Port-Channel
Port Map AI
Fiber Map AI
IB perf
nvidia-smi
Free-form AI
Power
LED / GPU
Runbook
Rack Map
Blast Radius
Thermal
PDU & Power Math
80% Derate Rule
NEC 210.20
▶

Never load a breaker above 80% continuously.

BreakerVoltageMaxUsable kW
30A 1Ø208V24A4.99 kW
60A 1Ø208V48A9.98 kW
30A 3Ø208V24A8.65 kW
60A 3Ø208V48A17.3 kW
100A 3Ø208V80A28.8 kW

kW = V × A × PF × √3 (3Ø) / 1000 · PF = 0.9–0.95 typical

GB300 NVL72 — 120kW DLC
120kW
▶
ParameterSpec
Peak rack power120 kW
CDU inlet temp max~45°C facility water
Flow rate15–30 LPM per rack
Fluid typeDI water or water-glycol
Electrical480V 3-phase typical
Do NOT power up GB300 without confirmed coolant flow. GPU damage in minutes at load without cooling.
DLC / CDU Troubleshooting
COOLING
▶
LEAK RESPONSE
1. Alert team lead immediately
2. Identify leak source (QD, hose, CDU fitting)
3. Shut off at CDU manifold valve
4. Do NOT power down unless liquid near electrical
5. Document and escalate per site SOP
SymptomCauseAction
High GPU temp, flow OKAir pocketBleed loop, check flow meter
CDU high outlet tempFacility water warmContact facilities
No flow alarmValve, pump, filterCheck CDU pump, delta-P
QD dripO-ring, not seatedReseat; if persists replace
3-Phase Load Balancing
▶

Balance L1/L2/L3. Imbalance >10% causes neutral current, transformer heating, UPS issues.

Phase L1: 28.3A ← OK
Phase L2: 27.9A ← OK
Phase L3: 18.2A ← IMBALANCED — redistribute
GPU Platform Power & PDU Reference
H100 SXM5 / H200 SXM5 — Power
H100/H200
▶
ParameterH100 SXM5H200 SXM5
GPU TDP each700W700W
GPUs per node88
GPU subtotal5,600W5,600W
Full system (typical)10,000–11,000W10,500–11,500W
CoolingAir (high-perf)Air (high-perf)
Supply voltage200–240V AC200–240V AC
Power Cords & Connectors
Cord TypePlugReceptacleRatingUse Case
C19 to L6-30PNEMA L6-30PIEC C20 on PDU30A 208V 1ØPrimary server power — most common HGX H100 cord
C19 to L6-20PNEMA L6-20PIEC C20 on PDU20A 208V 1ØLower draw accessories, OOB switches
C13 to C14IEC C14 (male)IEC C13 (female)10A max1U accessories, 1G OOB switches, KVMs. NOT for servers.
Phase Distribution Rule

H100/H200 nodes are dual-corded. Each cord should be on a different phase AND different PDU.

# Example: 4-node rack, dual-corded, two PDUs (PDU-A and PDU-B)
Node 1 Cord A: PDU-A L1 | Node 1 Cord B: PDU-B L2
Node 2 Cord A: PDU-A L2 | Node 2 Cord B: PDU-B L3
Node 3 Cord A: PDU-A L3 | Node 3 Cord B: PDU-B L1
Node 4 Cord A: PDU-A L1 | Node 4 Cord B: PDU-B L2
C19 = female connector (on the server). C20 = male (on the PDU). C13 is rated 10A — never use on a server PSU. Verify cord rating matches breaker rating.
GB200 NVL36 — Power
GB200
▶
ParameterSpec
GPU TDP each1,000W
GPUs per rack36 (Grace Blackwell pairs)
GPU subtotal~36,000W
Full rack (typical)65,000–70,000W
CoolingDirect Liquid Cooling — MANDATORY
Supply voltage480V 3-phase (typical US)
Power Cords & Connectors
ConnectorTypeRatingNotes
IEC 60309 5-pin3P+N+E twist-lock (blue = 200–250V, red = 380–480V)32A–125AStandard industrial 3-phase. Red = 480V at US sites.
Hubbell CS8365CNEMA L21-30 3-phase 208V30A 3Ø 120/208VUsed on some PDU inputs at 208V sites
HardwiredLugged termination in PDUPer breaker ratingAt 65-70kW most sites go hardwired — no plug/socket
At 65-70kW: a single phase trip takes the rack down. Every circuit must be documented before power-up. Verify PDU load at each phase before adding equipment. 80% derate applies at rack level, not just circuit level.
GB300 NVL72 — Power
120kW
▶
ParameterSpec
GPU TDP eachUp to 1,200W (workload dependent)
GPUs per rack72 (Grace Blackwell pairs)
GPU subtotalUp to 86,400W
Full rack (peak)120,000W (120kW)
CoolingDirect Liquid Cooling — MANDATORY. Air impossible.
Supply voltage480V 3-phase. Multiple feeds.
Typical feed200A 3-phase per rack (some configs 2x 100A)
Power Cords & Connectors
ConnectorTypeRatingNotes
IEC 60309 5-pin (red)3P+N+E 480V twist-lock63A–125APrimary 480V 3-phase feed. Red housing = 380–480V.
Hardwired lugDirect bus bar termination200A+Most GB300 deployments hardwired from switchgear. No plug.
C19/C20IEC C19 server to C20 PDU16A per cordIndividual component cords within the rack to in-rack PDU
Phase Mapping for GB300

GB300 NVL72 racks typically have multiple in-rack PDUs fed by 3-phase. Distribution across phases must be balanced and documented before power-up.

# GB300 PDU phase mapping example
In-rack PDU-A (left): Feed 1 L1 · Feed 2 L2 · Feed 3 L3
In-rack PDU-B (right): Feed 1 L2 · Feed 2 L3 · Feed 3 L1
# Never put all feeds on same phase
# Verify with facilities before energizing
DO NOT energize GB300 NVL72 without: (1) confirmed DLC flow and temp, (2) all PDU phase assignments verified and documented, (3) facilities sign-off on electrical capacity. 120kW from a cold start can trip a breaker before coolant flow stabilizes.
Power Cord Quick Reference
ALL PLATFORMS
▶
ConnectorGender RuleRatingColor Code
IEC C13Female — goes ON the device10A maxN/A — accessories only
IEC C14Male — on the PDU/socket10A maxN/A
IEC C19Female — goes ON the server PSU16AN/A
IEC C20Male — on the PDU16AN/A
NEMA L6-20PPlug (male) — end of cord20A 208V 1ØVaries by site
NEMA L6-30PPlug (male) — end of cord30A 208V 1ØVaries by site
IEC 60309 blueTwist-lock industrialUp to 125ABlue = 200–250V
IEC 60309 redTwist-lock industrialUp to 125ARed = 380–480V
Site cord color conventions vary — red does NOT always mean 208V. Always verify voltage at the receptacle with a meter before connecting equipment. Never assume color.
PDU Load Balance Diagrams
H100 SXM5 — PDU Load Balance
H100
▶
8x PSU · 208V · ~6.4kW per PSU at full load · Dual feed A+B · 3-phase balanced
ℹ Tap any PSU or phase label to highlight that circuit path. L1=cyan L2=magenta L3=gold. Balance target: ≤10% variance across phases.
H200 SXM5 — PDU Load Balance
H200
▶
8x PSU · 208V · ~7.2kW per PSU at full load · Dual feed A+B · 3-phase balanced
ℹ H200 draws ~12% more per PSU than H100. Verify PDU breaker rating before hot-swap. Same physical layout, higher thermal envelope.
GB300 NVL72 — PDU Load Balance
GB300
▶
12x PSU · 208V · ~10kW per PSU · Dual feed A+B · 3-phase · DLC required · 120kW total rack
GB300 NVL72 requires dedicated 3-phase 200A service per rack. DLC must be active before powering on. Never energize without confirming coolant flow.
GPU Server Faults
H100 SXM5 — Common Faults
H100
▶
SymptomCauseAction
GPU not in nvidia-smiPCIe, driver, powerCheck slot power, reseat, BMC logs
ECC uncorrectable risingDRAM faultnvidia-smi -q -d ECC, RMA
GPU throttlingCoolingnvidia-smi -q -d TEMPERATURE
NVLink errorsCable/NVSwitchnvlink fabric diag, check cable
XID in dmesgXID 79=Reset, 94=driverCheck code, correlate smi
nvidia-smi
nvidia-smi -q -d ECC | grep -i error
dmesg | grep -i xid
nvidia-smi nvlink --status
BMC / iDRAC / iLO / IPMI
OOB
▶
VendorBMCNotes
DelliDRACRacadm CLI
HPEiLOREST API
SupermicroIPMIipmitool compat
NVIDIA HGXBMCRedfish API
ipmitool -I lanplus -H <IP> -U admin -P <pw> sdr
ipmitool -I lanplus -H <IP> -U admin -P <pw> sel list
ipmitool -I lanplus -H <IP> -U admin -P <pw> power cycle
NVMe Drive Replacement
STORAGE
▶
✓
Confirm failed drive: slot LED + OS identification
✓
Verify hot-swap capable for this bay
✓
RAID: confirm array tolerates removal — not already degraded
✓
Run lsblk and nvme list to confirm slot mapping before pull
✓
Pull failed drive — note slot number
✓
Insert replacement — same form factor (U.2 / M.2 / E1.S)
✓
Confirm OS sees new drive: nvme list
✓
Initiate RAID rebuild if applicable
✓
Tag and bag failed drive: date, host, slot, symptom
LED Indicator Reference
Dell PowerEdge LEDs
DELL
▶
System Health (front panel)
LED StateMeaningAction
Solid BlueSystem healthyNone
Blinking BlueID mode activeSomeone is locating this server
Solid AmberDegraded — non-critical faultCheck iDRAC SEL for details
Blinking AmberCritical faultCheck iDRAC immediately
PSU LEDs (rear)
LED StateMeaning
Solid GreenPSU healthy, AC input good
Blinking GreenPSU initializing — normal during POST
Solid AmberPSU fault or AC input lost
Blinking AmberPSU communication fault with system
OffPSU not present or no AC input
DIMM / Drive LEDs
ComponentAmber LEDNo LED
DIMM slotDIMM fault on that slotHealthy (no LED on good DIMMs)
Drive bayDrive fault or predictive failureHealthy
Drive bay blinkBlinking = rebuild in progress
ℹ Dell fans have no LED. Fan health is software-reported via iDRAC: racadm -r <ip> getsensorinfo | grep Fan
HPE ProLiant LEDs
HPE
▶
System Health (front panel)
LED StateMeaningAction
Solid GreenSystem healthyNone
Solid AmberDegraded — non-criticalCheck iLO IML (Integrated Management Log)
Solid RedCritical faultCheck iLO immediately
Solid Blue (UID)Server being identifiedUID button activated — press to deactivate
Blinking Blue (UID)Remote iLO session activeNormal during remote management
PSU LEDs
LED StateMeaning
Solid GreenPSU healthy
Solid AmberPSU degraded or failed
OffPSU not present or no AC
DIMM Slots

HPE: Each DIMM slot has an amber LED on the system board. Amber = fault on that DIMM. Check iLO IML for correctable vs uncorrectable ECC details.

# Check iLO IML from OS
hponcfg -f getiml.xml
# From iLO CLI
show /system1/log1/entry*
Supermicro LEDs
SUPERMICRO
▶
System Health
LED StateMeaning
Solid Green (PWR)System powered and healthy
Solid Amber (HDD)Drive fault
Blinking Amber (HDD)Drive activity (normal) or rebuild
NIC LED Green/AmberGreen = link/activity, Amber = 1G (vs Green = 10G)
PSU (on PSU face)
LED StateMeaning
GreenPSU healthy, AC input present
Amber / RedPSU fault — replace
OffNo AC input or PSU failed
DIMM Slots

Supermicro board-level DIMM LED behavior varies by model. Not all boards have per-slot LEDs. Check IPMI SEL for memory errors: ipmitool sel list | grep -i mem. Board-level amber near DIMM bank = fault in that bank.

NVIDIA HGX / GB-series
NVIDIA
▶

HGX and GB-series boards have minimal external LEDs. GPU health is primarily software-reported.

WhatHow to Check
GPU healthnvidia-smi — all GPUs visible and no throttle
GPU errorsdmesg | grep -i xid
System health LEDOEM chassis (Wiwynn/Foxconn/QCT) — amber = fault
PSUCheck OEM chassis PSU indicators — same as standard server
NVLink statusnvidia-smi nvlink --status
BMC SELipmitool sel list — hardware events logged here
ℹ For GB300 NVL72: the NVL72 chassis has status LEDs on the front panel managed by the chassis BMC. Check NVIDIA NVL72 field service manual for chassis LED definitions — these vary from standard server LEDs.
Incident Note Template
Ticket-Ready Format
▶
## INCIDENT — [DATE] [TIME] [NAME]

Asset: [hostname / rack / port]
Reported by: [monitoring / user / self]
Impact: [# hosts / services]

## Symptoms
[What was observed]

## Investigation
1. [HH:MM] — [Action] — [Result]
2. [HH:MM] — [Action] — [Result]

## Root Cause + Resolution
[Cause] / [What restored service]

## Parts Used
[Part# / serial — OLD and NEW]
Change Management Checklist
✓
Pre: Change ticket approved, window confirmed
✓
Pre: Rollback plan documented
✓
Pre: Stakeholders notified
✓
Pre: Baseline captured — counters, ping, config backup
✓
During: Each step logged with timestamp
✓
During: Each step verified before proceeding
✓
Post: Service verified, change validated
✓
Post: Config saved to running + startup
✓
Post: Ticket updated and closed
✓
Post: DCIM / asset management updated
Escalation Decision Tree
ESCALATE IMMEDIATELY
• DLC leak near live electrical
• Multiple hosts / racks affected
• UPS or PDU fault
• GPU XID 94 on production cluster
• FEC uncorrectable > 0 on production uplinks
• Smoke, smell, physical damage
RESOLVE THEN DOCUMENT
• Single optic replacement
• Single drive hot-swap
• Cable re-seat / clean
• Single host reboot via BMC
Shift Handover
Shift Start Checklist
START OF SHIFT
▶

Run this at the start of every shift before touching anything.

✓
Review open tickets from previous shift — note anything active or pending
✓
Check monitoring console — any red/amber alerts that weren't there at last check
✓
Verify any ongoing maintenance windows — who owns them, what stage they're at
✓
Check environmental — temperature trending normal, no DLC anomalies
✓
Check parts on order — any deliveries expected this shift?
✓
Note any equipment in degraded state even if not actively alarming
✓
Confirm verbal handover received from outgoing tech on any P1/P2 issues
Shift Handover Template
END OF SHIFT
▶
━━ SHIFT HANDOVER ━━
Date/Time: [DATE] [HH:MM] → [HH:MM]
Outgoing: [YOUR NAME]
Incoming: [NEXT TECH NAME]

━━ OPEN INCIDENTS ━━
Ticket # Status Owner Next Action ETA
[JIRA-XXX] [STATUS] [NAME] [ACTION] [ETA]

━━ ACTIVE MAINTENANCE WINDOWS ━━
Window: [Description]
Stage: [Pre / In-progress / Post-verification]
Owner: [Name / Team]
Ends: [Time]

━━ ACTION REQUIRED THIS SHIFT ━━
[ ] [Task description] — [Priority] — [Context]
[ ] [Task description] — [Priority] — [Context]

━━ DEGRADED / WATCH LIST ━━
Asset: [Hostname / rack / port]
Issue: [What's degraded — not alarming but watch it]
Ticket: [Reference or "no ticket yet"]

━━ ENVIRONMENTAL NOTES ━━
[Anything running hot, DLC anomaly, humidity, etc.]

━━ PARTS / LOGISTICS ━━
[Parts on order, expected delivery, RMAs pending]

━━ HAPPENED THIS SHIFT / NO TICKET ━━
[Anything the next team should know that didn't generate a ticket]
P1 or P2 active during your shift = VERBAL handover required, not just written. Never hand off an active critical incident in a note alone.
Incident Severity Reference
P1–P4
▶
SeverityDefinitionResponseHandover
P1 — CriticalFull outage, production down, data loss riskImmediate — all handsVerbal mandatory
P2 — HighSignificant degradation, redundancy lost<30 min responseVerbal mandatory
P3 — MediumPartial impact, workaround available<4 hoursWritten OK
P4 — LowMinor issue, no immediate impactNext business dayWritten OK
ℹ Severity definitions vary by site. These are industry-standard baselines — follow your site-specific SLA matrix if one exists.
ESD Pre-Work Checklist
ESD · SAFETY
▶

Run this before touching any sensitive hardware — GPUs, DIMMs, NICs, NVMe, transceivers, PCIe cards. ESD damage below 3,000V is imperceptible but can degrade a $30k GPU that fails under load weeks later.

Before You Touch Anything
✓CheckDetail
□Wrist strap on skinMust contact bare skin — not over a sleeve
□Wrist strap continuity testedUse tester on strap before starting — straps fail silently
□Bonded to rack or ESD matClip to unpainted metal on rack — not a painted surface
□ESD mat deployed (if available)Required for bench work — optional in-rack if strapped
□Component still in ESD bagDo not open bag until ready to install
□Clear ESD-safe work surfaceNo cardboard, no regular plastic — both generate static
Component Risk Levels
ComponentRiskNotes
GPU (H100 / H200 / GB300)EXTREMEAlways strapped, always on ESD mat
DIMM / MemoryEXTREMEHandle by edges only, never touch PCB
NVMe / SSDHIGHHold by edges, avoid connector pins
NIC / ConnectXHIGHKeep in bag until moment of install
Transceiver / OpticMEDIUMAvoid touching the optical port pins
PSULOWStill strap — but less sensitive
Cable / DACMINIMALStandard handling OK
What NOT To Do
NeverWhy
Set bare PCB on cardboardCardboard tribocharges — generates kV of static
Wear strap over clothingNo skin contact = no ground path = useless
Bond to painted rack surfacePaint is an insulator — no continuity
Slide components on surfacesFriction generates charge — lift, don't drag
Work in low humidity (<30%)Dry air = high static risk — dry winter months especially
Remove from bag near powered gearInduced charge from nearby PSUs
If You Feel or See a Discharge
StepAction
1Stop work immediately — do not proceed
2Document: component, time, what was touched
3Tag the component — do not install
4Report to lead — component may need inspection or RMA
5Check your strap — replace if continuity test fails
ESD damage is cumulative and latent. A component that survives an ESD event may degrade over weeks and fail under thermal or electrical stress. When in doubt — tag it, don't install it.
Server
GPU
Switch
PDU
Storage
Patch
UPS
Other
ASHRAE Thermal Envelope
ASHRAE Class A1 / A2 — Data Center Standard
ASHRAE TC 9.9
▶

ASHRAE TC 9.9 defines thermal envelope classes for IT equipment. Most modern data center hardware is rated Class A2 or higher. Inlet temperature is measured at the front face of the equipment, not ambient room temperature.

Class Definitions
ClassInlet Temp RangeMax HumidityTypical Use
A115–32°C (59–89.6°F)80% RH non-condensingControlled enterprise DC
A210–35°C (50–95°F)80% RH non-condensingMost modern GPU servers
A35–40°C (41–104°F)85% RH non-condensingHigher-efficiency facilities
A45–45°C (41–113°F)90% RH non-condensingAggressive efficiency targets
ASHRAE class is a rating of the equipment, not the room. A server rated A2 can tolerate up to 35°C inlet — but operating at the ceiling reduces component lifespan and increases failure probability. Target inlet temp should be well below the class maximum.
GPU Platform Thermal Limits
CRITICAL
▶
PlatformCoolingMax InletGPU Junction MaxThrottle Onset
H100 SXM5Air30°C83°C~80°C junction
H200 SXM5Air30°C83°C~80°C junction
GB200 NVL36DLC required25°C coolant supply—Coolant flow loss = immediate throttle
GB300 NVL72DLC required22–25°C coolant supply—Coolant flow loss = immediate throttle
A100 SXM4Air30°C83°C~80°C junction
L40SAir35°C89°C~85°C junction
GPU junction temperature is not inlet temperature. A rack running at 28°C inlet can still have GPU junctions at 82°C under full load. Always check GPU temp via nvidia-smi or XID logs, not just room sensors.
GB200 and GB300 NVL systems use Direct Liquid Cooling. Loss of coolant flow at full load will cause immediate thermal throttling and potential XID thermal events within minutes. Never power up DLC systems without confirming coolant flow and supply temperature.
Thermal Alert — Action Thresholds
FIELD GUIDE
▶
Inlet Temperature Actions
Inlet ReadingStatusAction
≤27°CNormalNo action required
28–30°CWatchMonitor. Check CRAC/CRAH operation. Verify no hot aisle/cold aisle mixing.
31–34°CWarningNotify facilities. Check blanking panels. Reduce workload if possible. Escalate if sustained.
≥35°CCriticalImmediate facilities escalation. GPU throttling likely. Risk of thermal shutdown. Document and ticket.
GPU Junction Temperature Actions
Junction TempStatusAction
≤75°CNormalNo action
76–82°CElevatedMonitor. Check inlet temp and airflow. Verify no blocked cable paths in rear exhaust.
≥83°CThrottle riskCheck cooling immediately. Review nvidia-smi for throttle state. Notify on-call if sustained.
DLC / CDU Thermal Field Reference
GB200 · GB300
▶

Direct Liquid Cooling (DLC) systems use a Coolant Distribution Unit (CDU) to supply chilled water or coolant to server cold plates. For GB200 and GB300 NVL systems this is not optional — it is required for operation.

Key Parameters to Monitor
ParameterNormal RangeAction if Outside
Coolant supply temp18–25°CNotify facilities. Warm supply = reduced cooling capacity.
Coolant return tempSupply + 5–12°CHigh delta-T may indicate flow restriction.
Flow ratePer CDU specLow flow = immediate throttle risk. Check CDU pumps.
Coolant pressurePer CDU specPressure drop may indicate leak. Inspect manifolds.
DLC Fault Response
STEP 01
Confirm coolant flow at CDU
Check CDU status panel or BMS. Verify pump operation and flow rate.
STEP 02
Check for leaks at manifolds
Inspect quick-disconnect fittings at server manifold. Any moisture = immediate escalation to facilities.
STEP 03
Check supply temperature
Verify coolant supply temp is within 18–25°C range. Warm supply reduces effective cooling capacity.
STEP 04
Monitor GPU junction via nvidia-smi
nvidia-smi --query-gpu=temperature.gpu --format=csv,noheader — run per node to confirm thermal recovery.
Any coolant leak near energized equipment = immediate power-down and facilities emergency response. Do not attempt to clean or contain a coolant leak without confirming power is isolated. Coolant conducts electricity.
Hot Aisle / Cold Aisle — Field Checks
AIRFLOW
▶

Hot aisle / cold aisle containment separates cool supply air from hot exhaust air. Breakdowns in containment directly raise inlet temperatures even when CRAC/CRAH units are operating normally.

Common Causes of Thermal Elevation
CHECK 01
Missing blanking panels
Any empty rack U without a blanking panel allows hot exhaust to recirculate into the cold aisle. One missing 1U blank can raise local inlet temp by 2–4°C.
CHECK 02
Cable management blocking rear exhaust
Dense cable bundles routed across the rear of servers restrict hot air exhaust. Check that no cables block more than 20% of rear airflow area.
CHECK 03
Containment curtain gaps
Check aisle containment curtains and doors. Gaps allow hot exhaust to bypass containment and enter cold aisle supply.
CHECK 04
Under-floor grille placement (raised floor)
Verify perforated floor tiles are positioned in cold aisles only and not partially under hot aisle equipment.
ℹ Always measure inlet temperature at the front face of the equipment, not at the CRAC unit or room sensor. Room ambient can read 22°C while server inlet is 31°C due to containment failures.
